questions.question: What is the main challenge with HIIT for mental health populations according to the research?

options.text: HIIT has no mental health benefits at all
HIIT shows mood benefits in some populations but has high dropout rates in mental health populations due to intensity overwhelming capacity — adherence is POOR despite potential benefits
HIIT is the only effective exercise for mental health
HIIT is too easy and not challenging enough

questions.explanation: HIIT's major issue is the adherence paradox: while it can produce strong mood benefits in those who tolerate it (via endocannabinoid surge, cathartic release), dropout rates are high in mental health populations because the intensity is overwhelming. The lesson emphasizes that HIIT is NOT recommended as first-line for mental health — moderate-intensity cardio produces comparable benefits with better adherence.

questions.question: Why can HIIT be contraindicated (not recommended) for people with panic disorder or severe anxiety?

options.text: People with anxiety should never exercise at all
HIIT triggers intense physical arousal (elevated HR, rapid breathing, gasping) that mimics panic attack physiology — this can trigger actual panic in anxiety-prone individuals
HIIT cures panic disorder instantly
There are no contraindications for HIIT

questions.explanation: HIIT pushes you to 85-95% max HR with rapid breathing, heart pounding, and muscle burning. For people with panic disorder or severe anxiety, these intense physical sensations closely mimic panic attack physiology — which can trigger actual panic responses. The lesson is clear: if you have panic disorder, PTSD with somatic symptoms, or severe anxiety, choose moderate-intensity cardio or yoga instead.

questions.question: What is the recommended prerequisite before attempting HIIT?

options.text: You can start HIIT immediately with no preparation
Build an aerobic base first: 20-30 min moderate-intensity cardio, 3-4x/week for 4-8 weeks to build tolerance for elevated heart rate and breathing
You should only do HIIT if you're already an elite athlete
HIIT doesn't require any preparation or progression

questions.explanation: HIIT is an advanced modality. Jumping straight into it without a base = injury and burnout. The lesson recommends establishing 20-30 min moderate-intensity cardio, 3-4x/week for 4-8 weeks first. This builds tolerance for elevated heart rate and breathing, establishes cardiovascular fitness, and reduces injury risk. Then progress gradually: short intervals (30 sec hard / 90 sec easy) before attempting longer or more intense protocols.

questions.question: What is the maximum recommended frequency for HIIT sessions?

options.text: HIIT should be done daily for best results
2x per week maximum, with at least 48 hours recovery between sessions — more is not better; overtraining risk increases
HIIT should only be done once per month
There is no limit to HIIT frequency

questions.explanation: The lesson is clear: HIIT maximum 2x/week with at least 48 hours between sessions. HIIT is extremely demanding on the body and nervous system. More is NOT better — benefits plateau, and overtraining risk (injury, burnout, fatigue, mood worsening) increases. On non-HIIT days, do moderate cardio, yoga, strength training, or rest. Recovery is non-negotiable.

questions.question: On a scale of 1-10, if you scored 1-4 on 'how appealing does HIIT sound right now,' what should you do?

options.text: Force yourself to do HIIT anyway because it's the best exercise
Stick with moderate-intensity cardio or yoga — HIIT is NOT required for mental health benefits, and if it sounds terrifying/overwhelming, it's not the right modality for you
Give up on all exercise
Only elite athletes should exercise anyway

questions.explanation: The lesson emphasizes that HIIT is NOT required for mental health benefits. If HIIT sounds terrifying, overwhelming, or deeply unappealing, that's your body/brain telling you it's not the right fit. Moderate-intensity cardio produces comparable mental health benefits with better adherence and lower risk. The best exercise is the one you'll actually do — not the one that sounds most 'hardcore.'
